did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning approach based on a modified Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture, specifically VGG16 enhanced with the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), to classify skin images for melanoma detection. The model was trained using transfer learning and fine-tuning on a dataset of melanoma and non-melanoma images, achieving an accuracy of 91.25% and an F1-score of 91.13%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a modified VGG16-CBAM deep learning model for melanoma detection. It details specific methods including data collection from Kaggle and Kotaraja Health Center, model training with transfer learning, and reports original results such as 91.25% accuracy and 91.13% F1-score on a specific dataset, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma detection on skin images using deep learning based on convolutional neural network (CNN)
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning-based system for melanoma detection on skin images to address the scarcity of dermatologists in resource-limited settings like Papua, Indonesia. The researchers utilized a modified VGG16 Convolutional Neural Network enhanced with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), trained via transfer learning on a dataset of 2,036 images sourced from Kaggle and Kotaraja Health Center. The model was evaluated using a confusion matrix on 80 test samples, achieving an accuracy of 91.25%, precision of 92.31%, recall of 90%, and an F1-score of 91.13%. Additionally, the system incorporated Grad-CAM for interpretability and was deployed as a Flask-based web application to support healthcare practitioners. The results demonstrate reliable performance in classifying melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions, though the authors note that sensitivity requires improvement to minimize false negatives.

lesion_types_studied.details: Positive class in the binary classification task; 1,272 samples in the training dataset and 40 cases in the test set.

Negative class in the binary classification task; 1,272 samples in the training dataset and 40 cases in the test set. The paper does not specify the exact histological types (e.g., nevus, BCC) within this category.

all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions. The task is explicitly described as 'melanoma classification' and 'classifying skin diseases'. Performance was evaluated using a confusion matrix with True Positives (Melanoma predicted as Melanoma) and True Negatives (Non-melanoma predicted as Non-melanoma), along with metrics such as accuracy (91.25%), precision (92.31%), recall (90%), and F1-score (91.13%).

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesion images obtained from Kaggle, combined with local clinical data.

use.training: Used for training the modified VGG16-CBAM model via transfer learning and fine-tuning (70% of total data).
use.validation: Used for validation during model development (10% of total data).
use.testing: Used for final system testing via confusion matrix (20% of total data, specifically 80 samples: 40 melanoma and 40 non-melanoma).
use.other: Data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, zooming, etc.) were applied to this dataset to address class imbalance and reduce overfitting.

datasets_used.name: Kotaraja Health Center (Puskesmas Kotaraja) Dataset
datasets_used.description: Local clinical data collected through direct observation of patients treated at Kotaraja Public Health Center in Jayapura City, Indonesia, over one month.

use.training: Combined with the Kaggle dataset to form the total training/validation/testing pool.
use.other: Used for clinical tests to evaluate the system's performance in real-world conditions and gather feedback from medical personnel.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the performance of the developed system by counting True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, and False Negatives on 80 test samples.

statistical_methods_used.method: AUC-ROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's discriminative capability and robustness during validation.

statistical_methods_used.method: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, Sensitivity, False Positive Rate, False Negative Rate
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide a comprehensive view of the system’s performance in classifying detected diseases and to assess the balance between precision and recall.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, specificity, sensitivity) derived from a confusion matrix on a test set of 80 samples.

testing_split.description: 20% of total data (407 samples total, but final evaluation performed on a subset of 80 samples: 40 melanoma and 40 non-melanoma)

architecture_used.main_model: Modified VGG16 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from a VGG16 model pre-trained on ImageNet

classification_or_output_head.threshold: 0.5 (standard), suggested optimization to 0.4 for improved recall

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CNN with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Pixel values were rescaled as part of the augmentation process.

Mentioned in the introduction as a method for realistic synthetic lesion synthesis to optimize small datasets, though specific implementation details or results for this technique are not provided in the results section.

conclusions: The modified VGG16-CBAM deep learning model demonstrated reliable performance for melanoma detection with an accuracy of 91.25%, precision of 92.31%, recall of 90%, and an F1-score of 91.13% on the test dataset.
The model achieved high specificity (92.5%), indicating a low false positive rate, which helps reduce the risk of unnecessary biopsies.
The 10% false negative rate is identified as a significant limitation that could lead to delayed diagnoses, necessitating improvements in sensitivity for clinical reliability.
The system serves as an efficient, interpretable, and affordable diagnostic support tool for resource-limited settings, such as Papua, Indonesia, where dermatologists are scarce.
Future work should focus on increasing sensitivity to over 95% using weighted loss functions or cost-sensitive learning to address class imbalance.
Optimizing the classification threshold (e.g., lowering it to 0.4) is recommended to improve recall and minimize false negatives.
External validation using independent datasets from different hospitals is necessary to ensure the model's generalizability.
Integration with clinical systems via web-based tools or APIs is suggested to facilitate ease of use for healthcare professionals.
Exploration of alternative model architectures, such as EfficientNet or Vision Transformer, is recommended to potentially achieve better accuracy on dermatological data.
important_remarks: The final evaluation was performed on a very small test subset of only 80 samples (40 melanoma, 40 non-melanoma), which limits the statistical power and reliability of the reported metrics (91.25% accuracy, 90% recall).

The study lacks external validation; the dataset combines a public Kaggle dataset with local clinical data from a single health center (Kotaraja Health Center), raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or clinical settings.
A 10% false negative rate (4 missed melanoma cases) is identified as a critical clinical limitation, as delayed diagnosis of melanoma poses significant patient risk.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to assess the significance of the performance metrics.
The paper mentions StyleGAN-based augmentation in the introduction as a novelty but does not provide specific implementation details or results for this technique in the methods or results sections.
The model achieved high training accuracy (99.72%) compared to validation accuracy (95.28%), indicating some degree of overfitting despite the authors' claim of no overfitting.

The 'Non Melanoma' class is a heterogeneous group containing unspecified histological types (e.g., nevi, BCC, SCC), which may affect the model's ability to distinguish melanoma from specific benign or malignant mimics.
Deployment was limited to a Flask-based web application and Docker; there is no evidence of integration into actual clinical workflows or prospective clinical trials.
